text: BIHAR, a county of Upper Hungary, which derives its name from the once fortified town of Bihar. It is bounded by the counties of Saboltsch and Sathmar on the N; by that of Krassna, and the principality of Transylvania, on the E; by the county of Sarand on the S; and by Great Cumania, and the county of Bekesh, on the W. It lies between the parallels of 46° 28' and 47° 40'; and has an area of 200.3 German, or 4,253 English sq. m. The inhabitants, whose number amounted in 1837 to 444,430, are for the most part Hungarians, and are said to be superior to the rest of their countrymen, both in morals and in purity of language. The mountainous parts are inhabited by Wallachians. About 160,000 are Protestants; 140,000, Orthodox Greeks; 40,000, Catholics; and 2,500 Jews. The district is mountainous towards the E, where it is encircled by the Carpathians; towards the W it is flat and marshy. The principal rivers are the Black Korös and the Berettyo. The vegetable productions are wheat, Indian corn, hemp, flax, saffron, and tobacco. Delreczin is the cap. The co. is divided into the 5 circles of Sarete, Zalonta, Belenyes, Vardein, and Ermelye.
BIHBIHAN. See BEHABAN.
BIHRI. See BEHREE.

BIISK, a large village of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Tomsk, 60 m. SE of Barnaoul, on the river Bi, at its confluence with the Katunga, in the centre of a natural amphitheatre formed by the Altai mountains, and at an alt. of 5,000 ft. above the Caspian. Pop. 2,000.
BIJA, a river of Asiatic Russia, which issues from Lake Teletskoi, in the gov. of Kolhyvan, and joining the Katunia at Katunskaia, forms the great river Ob or Obi.

BIJAGUR, a celebrated fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, in N lat. 24° 37'. The fortifications are built round the edge of a perpendicular rock, but it was taken with little difficulty by the British in 1781, during the revolt of Cheit Sing.

BIJANAGUR, or BISNAGHUR, a decayed city of Hindostan, on the Tumbuddra river, in N lat. 15° 14'. That portion on the NW bank of the river is usually distinguished by the name Annagundy. The only means of communication between the two parts of the city is by a ferry. The whole buildings stand in a plain, over which are scattered immense granite rocks, many of which have been left in situ when building the city: the course of the streets having been occasionally altered to avoid their removal apparently. Strong walls, nearly 8 m. in circuit, surround that part of the city on the SE bank of the river; and near its W extremity is a magnificent temple dedicated to Mahadeva. Immense blocks of granite have occasionally been employed in the different architectural constructions in this city.—The city of B. was built about the middle of the 14th cent.; and in 1564 was sacked and destroyed by the combined forces of the sovereigns of Ahmednuggur, Beedar, Golconda, and Bejapore.
BIJAPUR. See BEJAPORE.
BIJBAHAR, or VIGIPARA, a town of India, in Cashmir, on the banks of the Jelum, 25 m. SE of Cashmir, in N lat. 33° 47'.
BIJIGHUR, a town and fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, in N lat. 27° 47'. It was taken by the British in 1803, after considerable resistance.

BIJNI, a principality of India; bounded by Butan on the N; Assam on the E; the Garrows on the S; and Rungpore on the W. It is intersected by the Brahmaputra river. The rajah is allowed to affect a kind of independency, although he pays a small tribute to the Rungpore collectorate.- The cap., of the same name, is in N lat. 26° 29'.
BIJORA. See BEZOARA.

BIJORE, a small district of Afghanistan, in the prov. of Cabul. Its cap., of the same name, is in N lat. 34° 47', E long. 71° 14'. It is situated 55 m. from the Indus. There is a tradition that the descendants of the Greeks, who accompanied Alexander to India, kept possession of this district for a considerable period.
BIJUCAY, a small island, in the sea of Mindoro, one of the Philippine group, in N lat. 10° 54'.
BIJUGAS. See Bissagos.

BIKANIR, a principality of Rajasthan, in Hindostan; bounded on the N by the territory of the Bhattis; on the E by Hurriana and Shekawatti; on the S by Judpur and Jeypur; and on the W by Jesalmir. Its area is about 18,000 sq. m. The surface is generally level, but rises in a cluster of sand-hills towards the centre of the territory. Fresh water is scarce; and the little which exists is greatly wasted by the intense evaporation which accompanies the hot winds. The crops therefore are scanty and always precarious. The only exports are madder, camels, bullocks, and horses. The revenue of the rajah does not exceed £50,000. In 1818 he was admitted under British protection. The principal towns are B., Rajgar, Chum, Nohar, and Bahaduran.

BIKANIR, the cap. of the above principality, is situated in N lat. 27° 57', 145 m. NNW of Ajmere. Its inhabitants have been estimated at 70,000. Most of the houses are mere huts; but it is surrounded by a pretty good wall, and a dry ditch.
BIKAR. See BEYHAR.
BIKBULAKOVA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Orenburg, in the circle of Ufa, on the river Ik, 80 m. NE of Orenburg.
BILAMAH. See BAHR BILAMAH.
BILASPUR. See BELASPUR.
BILAZAIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. and 6 m. SE of Thouars. Pop. 138.

BILBA'O, or BILBOA, Bello Vao, i. e., 'the Beautiful ford,'] a town of Spain, the cap. of Biscay proper, situated on the E or r. bank of the navigable river Nerva, or Ibaizabal, in N lat. 43° 15′, 6 m. ESE of Portugalete, and 73 ft. above sea-level, in a small but rich valley surrounded by high wood-clad mountains. The interjacent plain is well-cultivated, and covered with country-houses. It is well-built, has a spacious harbour, five parish-churches, five convents, two monasteries, an hospital, and 15,000 inhabitants. The houses are very high, and generally inhabited by several families, who occupy the different stories, as in Edinburgh; but contrary to the rule which obtains in the latter city, the highest stories are considered the best in B. A considerable part of the town is built on piles. It carries on an extensive commerce, the merchandise and wool of Spain being mostly exported through BIL this channel to England, France, Holland, and other countries; while the whole of the north of Spain is supplied, through it, with foreign merchandise. The amount of the yearly exports of wool from B. was at one time estimated at 50,000 or 60,000 sacks of 2 cwt. each. The other exports are wheat, iron, chestnuts, madder, olive oil, saffron, and wine. The iron mines of Veneras, within 5 m. of B., are extremely rich. The imports are stockfish, Baltic hemp, materials for ship-building, ship stores, cocoa, sugar, coffee, linen, English woollen and cotton manufactures, drugs, French wines, &c. In 1842, 167 vessels of a total tonnage of 18,898 tons arrived from foreign ports at B.; and the invoice value of their cargoes amounted to £160,050. Of these 43 vessels were British. The number of vessels, great and small, that visit the harbour yearly, is between 500 and 600; but only small vessels under 60 tons can sail up to the town, except with a very full tide. The greater part therefore remain at Olaveaga, 2 m. below the town. Numerous wharfs and moles have been formed on both banks of the river down to Portugalete. The mouth of the river is impeded by a shifting-bar having less than a fathom of water over it at low tide. Spring-tides rise about 13 ft. in the river. In consequence of the opposition of the Biscayans to the establishment of custom-houses, they were long deprived of direct intercourse with the Spanish colonies in America, so that it was necessary to fit out all their shipments for that quarter in Santander, or some port beyond the province. There were formerly a number of tanyards here, but they have, for the most part, gone to decay.—This town was founded in the year 1300, by Don Diego Lopez de Haro; it was several times taken and re-taken in the last peninsular war, and was the scene of extensive operations during the late war of succession in Spain, though as a military position nothing can be worse, being entirely surrounded by mountains which, on one side, command the town.
BILBOROUGH, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 4 m. WNW of Nottingham. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 267.
BILBROUGH, a parish in Yorkshire, 4 m. NE of Tadcaster. Area 1,410 acres. Pop. 216.
BILCZA, a town of Austria, in Gallicia, in the circle of Lemberg, on the Sered, 20 m. SE of Czortkow. Pop. 2,000.—Also a town of Poland, in the obwod of Kielce. Pop. 450.
BILDESTON, a parish in Suffolk, 14 m. WNW of Ipswich. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 857.
BILDHAUSEN, a hamlet of Bavaria, in the circle of Unter-Franken-Aschaffenburg, 6 m. from Munnerstadt. Pop. 60. A celebrated and wealthy abbey of Cistercians formerly existed here.
BILED. See Beled.

BILEDGIK, or BILEHJIK, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, in the sanjak of Sultanogi, on the N declivity of Olympus, midway between Brusa and Yenishehr. This town was surprised and taken by Osman in the year 1299.

BILEDULGERID, or BILAD-EL-JERID, [i.e. 'the Land of Dates,'] an appellation given to one of the divisions of Northern Africa, but which has been continually varying in the extent of its application. It was understood by the older geographers to comprehend all the countries formerly known by the name of Numidia; and was described as including the southern part of Algiers, with the whole tract of land between that kingdom and Egypt. It has been confined by De Lisle to a district situated to the S of Tunis, but, properly speaking, under the government of that state, from which it is separated by a ridge of high mountains. In later maps, and by the modern Arabs. the name Biledulgerid is applied to the whole of that district which lies between the maritime states of Barbary and the Sahara or Desert, extending from 29° to 33° N lat., and which comprehends Lower Suse, Dara or Draha, Taflet or Tafilelt, Segilmessa, and Biledulgerid Proper. This district may be described as bounded on the N by Tunis; on the E by Tripoli; on the S by Mount Atlas; and on the W by Algiers and the Sahara; and its area may be roughly estimated at 32,000 sq. m. This region, especially in the central parts, is sandy, barren, and mountainous, and almost entirely destitute of rivers and fresh water springs. In the districts nearer to the Atlantic, water may generally be found by digging 3 or 4 ft. below the surface, but it is of a brackish taste, and unwholesome quality. The climate is extremely hot and unhealthy; especially in the months of July, August, and September, when a suffocating wind, loaded with particles of sand, blows very tempestuously from the desert. In many parts of this extensive tract, considerable ruins are to be found, from which it would seem to have been formerly better peopled and more carefully cultivated; but its principal and almost only produce is dates. In some oases of the more western provinces, a little Indian corn, rice, wheat, and barley, are produced: in these also a superior breed of goats are reared which are in high repute among the inhabitants of Marocco. The natives of Biledulgerid are chiefly Arabs, originally from the Sahara, and who resemble the wandering tribes already described under the articles Arabia and Barbary. Those of them who border upon the northern states are more civilized and stationary in their habitations, frequently engaging in agriculture and the mechanic arts; but the inhabitants of the interior are a set of intractable banditti. The small-pox, and even the plague,—the great scourges of Barbary,—are said to be unknown in Biledulgerid, though the countries are contiguous, and the inhabitants have much intercourse. All these Arabs profess the religion of Mahommed; but have little knowledge of its tenets, and mix with it an endless variety of Jewish ceremonies and Pagan superstitions. The different tribes are under a species of nominal subjection to the neighbouring states of Algiers and Tunis, though it scarcely amounts to more than the payment of an annual tribute, which some of them even refuse to discharge.
BILESUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bejapore, 20 m. W of Satarah.
BILGA, a village of Persia, in the prov. of Azerdbijan, on the Maku river, 2 m. from the town of Maku.
BILGAM, or VILIGAM, a seaport of the island of Ceylon, situated on a bay, on the SW coast, in N lat. 5° 58'. Here is a celebrated pagoda.
BILGHEY, or BILGY, a town of Hindostan, in the Mysore, 35 m. NNW of Bednore, and 192 m. NW of Seringapatam.
BILGILLY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bejapore, 15 m. SW of Galgala.
BILGORAY, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Lublin, obwod and 20 m. SW of Zamosc, on the Lada. Pop. 3,100.
BILGUM, a town of Hindostan, in the circar of Cicacole, 25 m. NW of Cicacole.
BILHAM, a township in the p. of Hooton-Pagnell, in Yorkshire, 6 m. WNW of Doncaster. Pop. 75.
BILIARSK, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 90 m. SE of Kasan, on the Tcheremshane. Pop. 2,500.
BILIHAN, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Irak, 100 m. SSW of Hamadan.

BILIN, a small town of Bohemia, in the circle of Leitmeritz, with a castle belonging to the Prince of Lobkowitz. Pop. 3,200. It stands on the Bila, 15 m. W of Leitmeritz, and is celebrated for its mineral springs discovered in the early part of the 18th cent. Their waters are alkaline, and contain more carbonic acid than those of any other spring in Germany. BIL From 70,000 to 80,000 bottles are annually sent from the springs to various parts of Europe. There are immense strata of tripoli here. This stone is wholly made up of the siliceous cases and skeletons of microscopic animals; and Ehrenberg estimates that a cubic inch of the tripoli of B. contains the fossil remains of 41,000,000 of individuals of the Gaillonella distans.
BILINOV ISLES, a small group in the Arctic ocean, lying off the coast of the Russian prov. of Archangel, in N lat. 67°.
BILITZ. See BIELITZ.
BILLAPUR, a town of Hindostan, in Dowlatabad, 65 m. NW of Poonah, opposite Bombay.
BILLE, a small river of Germany, which rises in the bailiwick of Steinherst, in Lauenburg, and falls into the Elbe, 5 m. E of Hamburg, after a course of 35 m.
BILLE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Fougeres. Pop. 1,285.
BILLERICA, a township in Middlesex co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 18 m. NW of Boston, watered by the Concord and Shawsheen rivers, and intersected by the Boston and Lowell railroad.
BILLERICAY, a chapelry and market-town in the p. of Great Burstead, Essex, 23 m. ENE of London. Pop. 1,284.
BILLESDON, a parish and town in Leicestershire, 8 m. E of Leicester. Area of p. 4,430 acres. Pop., with the chapelries of Goadby and Rolleston, 878.
BILLESLEY, a parish in Warwickshire, 4 m. WNW of Stratford-on-Avon. Area 750 acres. Pop. 31.
BILLIEME, a village of Sardinia, in Savoy, 12 m. NW of Chambery. Pop. 1,500.

BILLIERS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Muzillac, on the r. bank of the Vilaine, at the embouchure of the Saint- Eloy, 32 m. SE of Vannes. Pop. 957. The entire tonnage entering this port in 1840 was 53 vessels of 1,626 tons burden.
BILLIGHEIM, a town of Bavaria, in the Pfalz circle, cant. of Bergzabern, on the Klingbach, 4 m. S of Landau. Pop. 1,755.
BILLING (GREAT and LITTLE), two parishes in Northamptonshire, the former 4 m. NE, the latter 3 m. ENE of Northampton. Area of Great B. 1,290 acres. Pop. 401. Area of Little B., 890 acres. Pop. 101.
BILLINGBOROUGH, a parish in Lincolnshire, 3 m. E by S of Folkingham. Area 2,020 acres. Pop. 999.
BILLINGE (CHAPEL END), a township in the p. of Wigan, in Lancashire, 5 m. SW of Wigan. Area 1,340 acres. Pop. 1,550.
BILLINGE (HIGHER END), a township in the p. of Wigan, in Lancashire, 41 m. WSW of Wigan. Area 1,410 acres. Pop. 712.
BILLINGFORD, a parish in Norfolkshire, 2 m. E of Scole. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 219.—Also a p. in Norfolkshire, 5 m. NNE of East Dereham. Area 1,650 acres. Pop. 353.
BILLINGHAM, a parish in the co. of Durham, 3 m. NNE of Stockton. Area 8,970 acres. Pop. 1,653, of whom 782 were in the township of B. Area of township 2,720 acres.
BILLINGHAY, a parish in Lineolnshire, 9 m. NE of Sleaford. Area 7,630 acres. Pop. 2,095.
BILLINGLEY, a township in the p. of Darfield, W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. E by S of Barnsley. Area 830 acres. Pop. 220.
BILLINGSGATE, an island at the entrance of Wellfleet bay, off the coast of Massachusetts, U. S., in N lat. 41° 51′ 38", W long. 70° 4′ 32". It has a fixed light upon it. ALCOHOLD TO THE
BILLINGSHURST, a parish in Sussex, 7 m. SW of Horsham. Area 6,830 acres. Pop. 1,439.
BILLINGSIDE, a township in the p. of Lanchester, 13 m. NW of Durham. Area 340 acres. Pop. 13.
BILLINGSLEY, a parish in the co. of Salop, 5 m. S by W of Bridgenorth. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. 149.
BILLINGTON, a chapelry in the p. and 2 m. SE of Leighton-Buzzard, in Bedfordshire. Pop. 323.
BILLINGTON (LANGHO), a chapelry in the p. and 6 m. NNE of Blackburn, in Lancashire. Area 2,960 acres. Pop. 988.

BILLITON, a rocky, sterile, quadrangular shaped island, in the Indian ocean, to the E of Banca, and SW of Borneo; 60 m. from the former; and separated from the latter by the strait of Carémata. It is about 50 m. in length, and 45 m. in breadth; and has a pop. of about 3,000 Malays, who are of industrious habits, and make excellent sailors. The island is well-timbered, and many of the Malay piratical prows are built here by the natives. Rice is the chief agricultural production. Trepang and edible birds' nests are exported. The great wealth of the island consists in its iron and tin mines. The ironore is peculiarly rich, and is used in the manufacture of the fine steel of which the Malays form their creases and edge-weapons.
BILLOCKBY, a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. NE of Acle. Area 260 acres. Pop. 71.

BILLOM, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of Clermont.— The cant. comprises 10 com., and had a pop. in 1831 of 14,267.—The town, 12 m. ESE of Clermont, is the cap. of the cant., and has a communal college, and some manufactories of thread, serges, and pottery. Pop. 4,746.
BILLSBOROUGH, a township in the p. of Garstang, Lancashire, 5 m. SE of Garstang. Area 750 acres. Pop. 157.
BILLY, a parish in co. Antrim, 6 m. NE of Coleraine. Area 17,330 acres. Pop. 7,277.
BILLY-BERCLAU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nievre, cant. of Cambrin. Pop. 1,435.
BILLY-CHEVANNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nievre, cant. of Saint-Benin-d'Azy. Pop. 1,158.
BILLY-SOUS-MANGIENNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Spincourt, on the Loison. Pop. 1,216.

BILMA, a town in the Sahara, 363 m. SSE of Murzuk, and 396 m. NNE of Bornu, in N lat. 21° 20', E long. 14° 20', on the route and midway between Fezzan and Bornu. It is a poor place, surrounded with low mud walls; but is regarded as the cap. of the Tibbus. It is situated towards the S extremity of the Wadi Kawas, which lies between two isolated rocky ridges, rising amidst the barren sands of what is called the Desert of B., which is connected on the NE with the Libyan desert; and stretches on the SE to the frontiers of Bornu. Caravans employ from 10 to 13 days in crossing this sand-ocean, through which the Tibbus steer their course by certain marks in the dark sandstone ridges, which here and there protrude through the sand.—About 2 m. N of the town of B. are several salt lakes, from which a large quantity of very fine crystallized salt is annually procured, which is chiefly sold in Bornu and Sudan .- Denham and Clapperton.

BILNEY (East and West), two parishes in the co. of Norfolk; the former 5 m. NNW of East Dereham; the latter 7 m. SE of King's Lynn. Area of East B. 670 acres. Pop. 218. Area of West B. 2,750 acres. Pop. 298. BIL of Khamul, turning off from the Tirishan, towards the S, between the Bugas and the Natsin.
BILOWES, a village of Bohemia, in the circle of Koniggratz, 2 m. W of Nachod, on the Mettau, near the frontiers of Silesia. Pop. 480. There are mineral springs here.

BILOXI, a village in Harrison co., in the state of Mississippi, U. S., 227 m. SE of Jackson, on a shallow bay of the same name, which sets off from Passagoula bay, on the gulf of Mexico, 10 m. W of the mouth of the Passagoula river.
BILSAH. See BHILSAH.
BILSBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 1 m. E of Alford. Area 2,960 acres. Pop., with the hamlets of Asserby and Thurlby, 584.
BILSDALE (MIDCABLE), a chapelry in Helmsley p., in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 12 m. NW by N of Helmsley. Area 8,380 acres. Pop. 738.
BILSDALE (WESTSIDE), a township in Hawnby p., in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. NW by N of Helmsley. Area 6,090 acres. Pop. 168.
BILSEN, a canton and town of Belgium, in Limburg, in the arrond. of Tongres, 8 m. W of Maestricht, on the l. bank of the Demer. Pop. 3,175. It has manufactures of cutlery and earthenware.
BILSHAUSEN, or BILLSHAUSEN, a town of Hanover, in the principality of Grubenhagen, bailiwick and 3 m. NW of Gieboldehausen, on the 1. bank of the Ruhme. Pop. 1,150.
BILSINGTON, a parish in Kent, 6 m. SSE of Ashford. Area 2,800 acres. Pop. 385.
BILSTAIN, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, 2 m. NE of Verviers. Pop. 600.
BILSTEIN, or BILLSTEIN, a village of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the circle of Olpe, 20 m. S of Arensberg. Pop. 400. There is a lead-mine in the vicinity.
BILSTHORPE, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 5 m. S of Ollerton. Area 1,600 acres. Pop. 244.

BILSTON, a market-town and chapelry in that part of the p. of Wolverhampton which is in the northern division of the hund. of Seisdon, Staffordshire; 3 m. SE of Wolverhampton; 19 m. S by E of Stafford; and 121 m. NW of London. Area of chapelry 2,580 acres. The numerous and rich mines of coal and iron-stone in the neighbourhood, have given rapid development to this place. It is situated in the immediate vicinity of the Birmingham and Stafford canal, by the various branches of which a communication is opened up to every part of the kingdom, through the Mersey, Dee, Ribble, Ouse, Trent, Derwent, Severn, Humber, Avon, and Thames; while by the opening of a new line—which is conducted through a noble tunnel at Cosely, near B.—the direct canal route between Birmingham and Wolverhampton has been shortened 4 m. The Liverpool and Birmingham Grand Junction railway also passes within 13 m. of B.; the nearest station on the line being that of Willenhall, which is 851 m. from Liverpool. Favoured by all these circumstances, B. is now a large and thriving town, and its vicinity is filled with forges, furnaces, steam-engines, and manufactories, the smoke of which darkens the air by day, while the flames illuminate the country by night. The principal manufactures of B. consist in the heavier description of iron castings, machinery of all kinds, tin-plate, japanned and enamelled wares, nails, wire, and screws. Clay of a coarse kind, used for making pottery, and a kind of deep orange-coloured sand which is found very useful in casting, are procured in abundance in the environs. Coal-mines are numerous and productive; at Bradley, in the immediate vicinity, a stratum of coal about 4 feet in thickness, has been burning for above fifty years. Bilston unites

BILNIDABAN, a mountain in China, 50 m. NE | with the township of Wolverhampton in returning two members to parliament. Pop. in 1801, 6,914; in 1831, 14,492; in 1841, 20,181. Assessed property in 1815, £15,634; in <math>1842-3</math>, £70,955.
BILSTONE, a township in the p. of Norton-juxta- Twycross, Leicestershire, 3 m. NW of Market-Bosworth. Area 570 acres. Pop. 857.
BILTEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 7 m. NW of Glaris, on the l. bank of the Liuth. Pop. 689.

BILTON, a parish in Warwickshire, 2 m. SW of Rugby. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. 623.—Also a township in Lesbury p., in Northumberland. Pop. 121.- Also a chapelry in Swine p., in the E. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,120 acres. Pop. 84.—Also a chapelry, with Harrogate, in Knaresborough p., in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 4,800 acres. Pop. 3,372.—Also a parish and township in the ainsty of the city of York, in Yorkshire, 5 m. NE of Wetherby. Area of p. 4,150 acres; of t. 1,460 acres. Pop. of p. 881; of t. 214.
BILWARA. See BHILWARA.

BIMA, a district and town in the island of Sumbawa, in the Asiatic archipelago, situated on a bay of the same name, remarkable for the magnificence of the surrounding scenery, in S lat. 8° 22', E long. 118° 40'. It has a port, but an extensive bank of sand renders it difficult of access. B. is populous, and possesses a considerable commerce in rice, pistachio-nuts, sapan-wood, wax, and horses. The district of B. is under the gov. of a sultan, allied to the Dutch East India company, who have an agent here. Pop. in 1811, 80,000.
BIMAH. See BHIMA.
BIMASSAR. See BEEMASIR.
BIMBIA, a river of West Africa, which flows into the bight of Biafra, in N lat. 4°, E long. 9° 20'.
BIMBUR. See BHIMBER.
BIME. See KANGRA.

BIMLIAPATAM, or BHIMALAPATANA, a small seaport town of Hindostan, in the Northern Circars, in the district and 7 m. NNE of Vizagapatam, on a stream which flows into the gulf of Bengal 8 m. SSE of Vizianagram. It contains a few good houses; and possesses a tolerably active trade, chiefly in cotton fabrics of local manufacture. A mountain rises to the S of the town, on which there is a temple.
BIMME. See Bemini.

BINAB, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Azerdbijan, near the E bank and on a tributary of Lake Shahee, or Uramia, 15 m. W of Maraga, and 55 m. SW of Tabriz. BINABOLA, BURABOLA, or TWELVE PINS, a grand and picturesque group of mountains, in co. Galway. It is situated between the head of Lough Corrib and Aghris point, and between Birterbuy and the Killeries; and occupies a space of about 25 sq. m.; or, if viewed as including offshoot-hills on the N and W, it forms an area of from 49 to 64 sq. m. It consists of two distinct ranges of summits, connected by the elevated pass or neck of Maam Ina. The names and altitudes of the principal summits, as ascertained by the Ordnance survey, are Benlettery, 1,904 ft.,—Bencullagh, 2,084 ft.,—Bencorr, or Bengower, 2,336 ft.,—and Benbaun, 2,395 ft. Quartz is the chief rock; it is in general distinctly stratified or at least schistose; it is usually grey or brown, especially where its stratification is very distinct, and sometimes reddens into sinople; but it is frequently massive, and traversed by veins of milk quartz and rock crystal; and, in most cases, it constitutes precipitous descents, and exhibits a great aggregate of bare rock. The cliff on the south side of Glen Ina is about 1,200 ft. high, and flings down a considerable stream in a stupendous waterfall. Limestone occurs to a considerable bulk in some places at BIN the foot of the mountains, and in patches or shieldshaped formations in lofty situations north of Glen Ina.
BINACRE, a parish in Suffolk, 6 m. NE of Southwold. Area 1,660 acres. Pop. 194.
BINARHISSAR, a town of Turkey in Europe, in of Romelia, in the sanjak and 18 m. NW of Viza.
BINAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-et-Cher, cant. of Ouzouer-le-Marche. Pop. 1,100.

BINASCO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, the cap. of a dist. of the same name, in the gov. and 10 m. SSW of Milan, on the canal of Pavia. It has a strong castle; but was taken by the French in 1796. Pop. 4,200.
BINBANE, a mountain on the N border of the p. of Inver, co. Donegal, on the r. side of the road from Mount Charles to Glenties. Alt. 1,490 ft.
BINBROOK, two parishes and a village in Lincolnshire, 8 m. NE of Market-Raisen, on the river Aucklam. Area of both p. 6,070 acres. Pop. 1,209.

BINCHE, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, on the r. bank of the Haine, 14 m. W of Charleroi, and 9 m. ESE of Mons. Pop. in 1837, 4,967. The town, which is the cap. of the cant., contains a college; and has a fine terraced esplanade on the site of a magnificent castle built by the regent Mary, but destroyed by the French in The manufacture of shoes and of lace is 1554. carried on here to a great extent: the former giving employment to 400 persons, and the latter to 600. There are also glass-works, tanneries and currieries, soap-works, salt-refineries, manufactories of hosiery, cutlery, and delft-ware, and tile-works. Its trade consists chiefly in lace, thread, paper, marble, and coal.
BINCHESTER, a township in the p. of St. Andrew-Auckland, in the co. of Durham, 1 m. N by E of Bishop-Auckland. Area 500 acres. Pop. 43.
BINCOMBE, a parish in Dorset, 4 m. S by W of Dorchester. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. 170.
BINDERTON, a township and chapelry in Sussex, 3½ m. N by W of Chichester. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. 75.

BINDIACUL, or BINDHYACAL, a mountain-range of Hindostan, in the district of Rewah, in the prov. of Allahabad, running from Kesogar N of Secundah, on the Sindh, by Narwar, Kalangar, and Tarapur, to the Ganges at Rajmahal. Its greatest alt. is 2,000 ft.; but its average elevation does not exceed 800 ft.
BINDLOES ISLAND, one of the Galapagos group, in the Pacific ocean, in N lat. 0° 15', and W long. 90° 33'.
BINDORF. See BENDORF.

BINDRABUND, or VRINDAVANA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 40 m. NNW of Agra, on the r. bank of the Jumna, in N lat. 27° 37', and E long. 77° 41'. This place—the name of which Vrindavana signifies 'a grove of tulsi trees'—is celebrated as the scene of the earlier exploits of the Hindu god Krishna, and numerous temples dedicated to his worship are found within its precincts. Of these the great cruciform pagoda is one of the finest edifices in Hindostan.
BINEGAR, or BINAGER, a parish in Somersetshire, 4 m. N of Shepton-Mallet. Area 680 acres. Pop. 338.
BINFIELD, a parish in Berks, 3 m. NE of Wokingham. Area 3,530 acres. Pop. 1,242.
BINGAH, or BHINGAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov of Oude, near the l. bank of the Rapty, 70 m. NE of Lucknow.
BINGARO, an islet of the Indian ocean, in the Laccadive group, in N lat. 10° 50′, and E long. 72° 28'. It is surrounded by a coral reef which extends about 2 m. in a NW direction.
BING INLET, a river of Upper Canada, in the Chippaway hunting country, which falls into Georgian bay, in N lat. 45° 47', and W long. 80° 28'.

BINGEN, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. and on the l. bank of the Rhine, at the confluence of the Nahe, 14 m. W of Maynz; at an alt. of 270 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1837, 4,415, of whom 420 were Jews, 221 Protestants, and the remainder Catholics. B. contains a church, a school, and 4 hospitals; and possesses manufactories of flannel, fustians, and tobacco, and numerous tanneries. The advantages of its situation render it the entrepot of an active transit trade. Its chief exports are wine, the produce of the environs, grain and cattle. Fairs are held twice a-year, and a market every week. This town, the Bingium of the Romans, was to a great extent destroyed by the French in 1689. The principal relic of its former importance is the castle of Klopp, the ruins of which still exist.—A little below B., at the entrance of the narrow gorge formed by the masses of the Taunus and Hundsruck is the Binger-see, or 'Hole of Bingen'-a rocky bar over which the Rhine descends with great impetuosity, and which, notwithstanding extensive works which have been undertaken for its amelioration, renders the navigation at low water extremely dangerous. Above this fall, on a rock in the middle of the stream, is the Mausthurm, or 'Rattower,' an edifice of the Middle ages of legendary renown.
BINGFIELD, a chapelry in the p. of St.-John-Lee, Northumberland, 6 m. NNE of Hexham. Pop. 111.
BINGHAM, a parish and market-town in the co. of Nottingham, 10 m. E of Nottingham, in the centre of the rich vale of Belvoir. Area of p. 2,930 acres. Pop 1,998.

BINGHAM, a township of Maine, U.S., in Somerset co., 60 m. N of Augustus, on the E side of Kennebec river. Pop. in 1840, 751.—Also a township of Pennsylvania, in Potter co., 181 m. NW of Harrisburg. Pop. in 1840, 453.

BINGHAMPTON, a village of New York, U. S., cap. of Broome co., 138 m. SW of Albany, at the junction of the Chenango and Susquehanna, both of which are crossed by bridges. It is intersected by the New York and Erie railways, and connected by the Chenango with the Erie canal. The surrounding dist, is extremely fertile. Pop. 2,800.
BINGHAM'S TOWN, or SALEEN, a village in co. Mayo, on a small creek on the W side of Blacksod bay, 3 m. SSW of Belmullet. Pop. 436. There is a small fishing-boat harbour here.

BINGLEY, a p. and town in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 14 m. W by N of Leeds. Area of p., 13,180 acres. The town stands on elevated ground, on the E bank of the Aire, and is one of those places now abounding in the N of England, which exhibit the rapid change of an insignificant village into a busy thriving town, under the influence of well-conducted manufacturing enterprise. Worsted mills, cottonmills, and paper-mills give employment to a great proportion of the pop. The railway from Shipley to Keighley passes B.

BINGO, or PI-HEOU, a prov. of Japan, near the SW extremity of the island of Nifon; bounded on the N by the prov. of Itsumo and Fuki; on the E by the prov. of Bitieou; on the S by the Swounada sea; and on the W by the prov. of Aki. It is generally fertile, and produces rice in large quantities. The cap., Fuko-yama, or 'Mountain of Happiness,' is situated on the S coast. Several of the small adjacent islands belong to the prov. of B.
BINGUT (CAPE), a promontory on the N coast of Africa, 53 m. E of Algiers, in the prov. of Beni Selim, in W long. 3° 55'. BIN
BINHAM, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. NE of New Walsingham. Area 2,200 acres. Pop. 502.
BINHAR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, in the dist. and 36 m. SE of Kuttack, on the Olonka, one of the mouths of the Mahanuddy.

BINIC, a commune and fishing-port of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, and cant. of Etables, on the straits of Dover, at the mouth of the Ic. Pop. 1828. The port has an active coast-trade, and is much frequented by fishing-boats. Its tonnage in 1840 amounted to 45 vessels, and 3,951 tons.
BENICICAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, district and 43 m. S of Sumbulpur, near the r. bank of the Mahanuddy.
BINLEY, a parish in Warwick, 2 m. E of Coventry. Area 2,470 acres. Pop. 233.—Also a tything in the p. of St. Mary Bourne in Hants. Pop. 138.

BINNEN, or BINNA, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, and bailiwick of Goms, in the valley and on the river of the same name, 6 m. ESE of the confluence of the latter with the Rhone, at an alt. of 3,380 ft. above sea-level. The cheeses of this district are esteemed the best in the cant. Pop. 205, Catholics.
BINNEN SEE, a lake of Prussia, in the prov. of Pomerania, forming the estuary of the Becknitz, and separated from the Baltic by the peninsula of Darass and Zingst island.
BINNI, a town of W Africa, in the kingdom of Bambarra, on the N bank of the Niger, 30 m. SSW of Sego.
BINNINGTON, a township in the p. of Willerby, 8 m. SW by S of Scarborough. Area 910 acres. Pop. 61.
BINOA, a river of India, in the Punjab, flowing into the Bias in N lat. 31° 45', after a SE course of about 50 m.
BINSDORF, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Black forest, and district of Sulz. Pop. 954.
BINSEY, a parish in Oxfordshire, 2 m. NW of Oxford, on the W bank of the Isis. Area 470 acres. Pop. returned with that of Oxford liberty. .
BINSTEAD, a parish in the isle of Wight, 6 m. ENE of Newport. Area 1,140 acres. Pop. 278.
BINSTED, a parish in Hants, 4 m. NE of Alton. Area 7,060 acres. Pop. 1,055.—Also a parish in Sussex, 2 m. W by S of Arundel. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 111.

BINTANG, an island in the Sunda archipelago, at the S extremity of the peninsula of Malacca, and to the SE of the island of Singapore, in N lat. 1° 6'. It is about 24 m. in length, and 12 m. in breadth; and has a pop. of about 20,000, of whom two-thirds are Chinese. It is well-cultivated, and produces large quantities of pepper; but its most important article of commerce is gambier, or Terra Japonica. The Dutch East India company have a resident at Riouv, the chief town on the island.
BINTENNE, a town of Ceylon, on the Mehavilla Gunga, 30 m. E of Kandy.
BINTON, a parish in Warwickshire, 4 m. W by S of Stratford-on-Avon. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 269.
